返回本批次

约翰·科尔特兰阐释了爵士乐的数学原理

John Coltrane illustrates the mathematics of jazz

www.americanjazzmusicsociety.com 作者 luu 分数 108 评论 14 2026/04/07 13:58
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. -via openculture.com 物理学家和萨克斯管演奏家斯蒂芬·亚历山大在他的许多公开演讲和他的《物理学爵士乐》一书中指出，阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦和约翰·科尔特兰有很多共同点。亚历山大特别提请我们注意所谓的“Coltrane 圆”，它类似于任何音乐家都会认为的“五度圆”，但融合了 Coltrane 自己的创新。 Coltrane 于 1967 年将这幅画送给了萨克斯管演奏家兼教授 Yusef Lateef，后者将其纳入了他的开创性著作《音阶和旋律模式库》中。正如拉蒂夫在自传中所写，他将科尔特兰的音乐视为一次“精神之旅”，“包含了丰富的自我生理心理音乐传统的关注”，而亚历山大则认为“与激发爱因斯坦量子理论的几何原理相同”。这两种描述似乎都不合适。音乐家兼博主罗尔·霍兰德 (Roel Hollander) 指出，“塞隆尼斯·蒙克 (Thelonious Monk) 曾经说过，‘所有音乐家在潜意识中都是数学家。’而像约翰·科尔特兰 (John Coltrane) 这样的音乐家则非常了解音乐数学，并有意识地将其应用到他的作品中。”科尔特兰也非常了解爱因斯坦的工作，并喜欢经常谈论它。音乐家大卫·阿姆拉姆 (David Amram) 记得巨人阶梯天才告诉他，他“正在尝试在音乐中做类似的事情。”霍兰德在两篇理论性很强的文章中仔细剖析了科尔特兰的数学，一篇概括地讨论了科尔特兰的“音乐与几何”，另一篇专门讨论了他的“音圈”。科尔特兰本人很少公开谈论他最著名的作品背后的密集理论工作，可能是因为他更希望它们自己说话。他更喜欢以哲学和神秘的方式表达自己，同样利用他对科学和各种精神传统的迷恋。正如爵士乐音乐家 Corey Mwamba 在 Facebook 上对其他几位演奏者进行非正式民意调查时发现的那样，Coltrane 充满诗意的说话方式让他的音乐诠释者能够以多种方式来看待他的 Circle。例如，单簧管演奏家阿伦·戈什（Arun Ghosh）在科尔特兰的“数学原理”中看到了“与神圣相连的音乐系统”。他认为，这是一个“让我感觉非常伊斯兰”的系统。拉蒂夫表示同意，而且可能很少有人比他更理解科尔特兰的方法。他与 Coltrane 密切学习多年，自 2013 年 Coltrane 去世以来，人们一直以他的同伴甚至导师的身份铭记他，尤其是他对世界各地的理论和音乐的普世拥抱。拉蒂夫甚至认为，如果不是因为担心“政治反弹”，科尔特兰晚年的杰作《至尊之爱》可能会被命名为“至高无上的真主”。有些人可能会觉得这种说法有倾向性，但我们从对科尔特兰音乐理论的广泛回应中看到，正如拉特夫所写，他对“音乐结构”的认识对他来说既是关于科学发现，也是关于宗教体验。对他来说，这两个过程都是直观的过程，“通过从经验中抽象出来，在音乐家的脑海中出现”，拉特夫写道。

Original Summary

  1. -via openculture.comPhysicist and saxophonist Stephon Alexander has argued in his many public lectures and his book The Jazz of Physics that Albert Einstein and John Coltrane had quite a lot in common. Alexander in particular draws our attention to the so-called “Coltrane circle,” which resembles what any musician will recognize as the “Circle of Fifths,” but incorporates Coltrane’s own innovations. Coltrane gave the drawing to saxophonist and professor Yusef Lateef in 1967, who included it in his seminal text, Repository of Scales and Melodic Patterns. Where Lateef, as he writes in his autobiography, sees Coltrane’s music as a “spiritual journey” that “embraced the concerns of a rich tradition of autophysiopsychic music,” Alexander sees “the same geometric principle that motivated Einstein’s” quantum theory.Neither description seems out of place. Musician and blogger Roel Hollander notes, “Thelonious Monk once said ‘All musicians are subconsciously mathematicians.’ Musicians like John Coltrane though have been very much aware of the mathematics of music and consciously applied it to his works.”Coltrane was also very much aware of Einstein’s work and liked to talk about it frequently. Musician David Amram remembers the Giant Steps genius telling him he “was trying to do something like that in music.”Hollander carefully dissects Coltrane’s mathematics in two theory-heavy essays, one generally on Coltrane’s “Music & Geometry” and one specifically on his “Tone Circle.” Coltrane himself had little to say publicly about the intensive theoretical work behind his most famous compositions, probably because he’d rather they speak for themselves. He preferred to express himself philosophically and mystically, drawing equally on his fascination with science and with spiritual traditions of all kinds. Coltrane’s poetic way of speaking has left his musical interpreters with a wide variety of ways to look at his Circle, as jazz musician Corey Mwamba discovered when he informally polled several other players on Facebook. Clarinetist Arun Ghosh, for example, saw in Coltrane’s “mathematical principles” a “musical system that connected with The Divine.” It’s a system, he opined, that “feels quite Islamic to me.”Lateef agreed, and there may be few who understood Coltrane’s method better than he did. He studied closely with Coltrane for years, and has been remembered since his death in 2013 as a peer and even a mentor, especially in his ecumenical embrace of theory and music from around the world. Lateef even argued that Coltrane’s late-in-life masterpiece A Love Supreme might have been titled “Allah Supreme” were it not for fear of “political backlash.” Some may find the claim tendentious, but what we see in the wide range of responses to Coltrane’s musical theory, so well encapsulated in the drawing above, is that his recognition, as Lateef writes, of the “structures of music” was as much for him about scientific discovery as it was religious experience. Both for him were intuitive processes that “came into existence,” writes Lateef, “in the mind of the musician through abstraction from experience.”

评论（默认中文）

mellosouls 2026/04/08 08:20
原始文章应该是链接（2017 年顺便说一句）：

https://www.openculture.com/2017/04/the-tone-circle-john-col...

Original article should be the link (2017 btw):

https://www.openculture.com/2017/04/the-tone-circle-john-col...

在 HN 中查看
rectang 2026/04/08 02:44
Coltrane 的变化是巨大的，但与多年来爵士乐和其他传统中的其他和声创新的规模相比。 他们与爱因斯坦不同。

《Giant Steps》之所以如此令人惊叹，是因为这些变化的流逝速度之快——如果你放慢速度，它与其他爵士乐曲调并没有什么不同。 Coltrane 经过多年的练习才掌握了在巨型台阶上即兴表演的专业技能。

The Coltrane changes are great, but on the scale of other harmonic innovations over the years inside Jazz and other traditions. They aren't analogous to Einstein.

What makes Giant Steps so amazing is the sheer speed at which those changes go past — if you slow it down, it's not that different from other Jazz tunes. It took took years of practice for Coltrane to acquire the specialized skillset for improvising over Giant Steps.

在 HN 中查看
evanb 2026/04/08 06:43
我不太记得音乐理论，但我对对称性了解得足够多，知道图中 9 点钟方向有一个错误。
I don't remember much about music theory but I know enough about symmetry to know that there's a mistake in the diagram at 9 o'clock.
在 HN 中查看
navane 2026/04/08 08:13
是的，但仅限于心形圆圈。心形的中间是经典的五度圈。在这个圆圈中，它们显示了音阶中半音的位置。
Yeah but only in the heart shaped circles. The middle of the hearts are the classic circle of fifths. In this circle they show where the half steps in the scales are.
在 HN 中查看
taylorbuley 2026/04/08 02:09
我注意到这是一个双环，而不是一个圆圈。两个同心色环，偏置。这不是装饰：外环和内环是在相位偏移处读取的相同字段（看起来像三全音/小三度旋转）。容错！
I notice it's a double ring, not a single circle. Two concentric chromatic rings, offset. That's not decoration: the outer ring and inner ring are the same field read at a phase offset (looks like a tritone / minor-third rotation). Fault tolerance!
在 HN 中查看
SoleilAbsolu 2026/04/08 02:47
实际上看起来是两个同心的全音环，而不是半音阶。
Actually appears to be 2 concentric whole-tone rings, not chromatic.

在 HN 中查看
cloudfudge 2026/04/08 04:47
作为一个真正热衷于音乐理论的人，我总是对那些我认为是基于数学的音乐理论的居高临下的虚假赞扬感到恼火。 这不是数学；而是数学。这是周期性模式。 是的，这一切都可以用数学来表示，令一些人感到惊讶的是，有感觉的东西能够以意想不到的规则方式映射到这些有趣的离散值循环，并且涉及非常有趣的数学比率，但这并不意味着它是数学的。 我认为我们不需要拍拍约翰·科尔特兰的头来谈论他的表现 实际上 有点聪明，因为他是 做数学。
As someone who's really into music theory, I am always annoyed by what I perceive as a patronizing faux exaltation of it supposedly being mathematically based. It's not math; it's cyclical patterns. Yes, it can all be represented mathematically, and it is surprising to some people how something with feeling can map to these interesting cycles of discrete values in unexpectedly regular ways, and there are very interesting mathematical ratios involved, but that doesn't make it math. I don't think we need to pat John Coltrane on the head and talk about how he's actually kind of smart because he's doing math.
在 HN 中查看
parpfish 2026/04/08 10:42
相同的。如果这也算数学的话，那么任何事物都与数学有关。一切事物都有可以描述的模式或结构。
Same. If this counts as math, anything and everything is connected to math. Everything has patterns or structure that can be described.
在 HN 中查看
aaplok 2026/04/08 05:53
事实上，我认为数学和爵士乐在公众的认知中有一些共同点，即你必须聪明才能“掌握它”。

没有人会尝试像分析 Coltrane 或 Miles Davis（或莫扎特或斯特拉文斯基）那样对 Lady Gaga 或 Ed Sheeran 的作品进行深入的智力分析。这些音乐家是爱因斯坦那样的知识分子，与 Lady Gaga 或 Ed Sheeran（在集体看法中）不同。爵士乐是一种理智的音乐。

当“聪明”的人分析某些东西时，他们会使用数学。

我在这里用“聪明”一词来吓人，以坚持认为这在很大程度上是一种社会认知和预期行为。然而，数学 能 明智地用于分析艺术，就像它在其他地方使用一样。这并不是居高临下，更多的是数学提供了一种有用的语言来讨论模式。

Actually I think that maths and jazz have something in common in the general public peception that you have to be smart to "get it".

Nobody will try to perform a deep intellectual analysis of Lady Gaga's or Ed Sheeran's work the way they analyse Coltrane or Miles Davis (or Mozart, or Stravinsky). Those musicians are intellectuals of the sort Einstein is, unlike Lady Gaga or Ed Sheeran (in the collective perception). Jazz is intellectual music.

And when they analyse something, "smart" people use maths.

I am putting scare quotes around "smart" here to insist that this is largely a social perception and expected behaviour. However, maths can sensibly be used to analyse art, just like it's used elsewhere. This is not patronising, it is more that maths provides a useful language to talk about patterns.

在 HN 中查看
Pay08 2026/04/08 05:32
啊，这就是化学烹饪。
Ah, so it's the cooking of chemistry.
在 HN 中查看
fc417fc802 2026/04/08 09:33
这是一个很好的比较，它提出了一个有趣的问题。在烹饪时，对化学技术的基本了解通常会非常有用，但在音乐方面，我不太确定数学。也许一些电子艺术家自己编写工具？
That's an excellent comparison and it raises an interesting question. When cooking a basic understanding of chemistry techniques will generally prove quite useful but when it comes to music I'm not so sure about math. Maybe some electronic artists who write their own tools?

I mean, we also don’t need to see something that’s not there. Also, I see you OP. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47645844

moogly 2026/04/08 06:24

Odd to mention Einstein and not Slonimsky, whose work he studied a lot and built upon.
